svg.aria-label: Conventional MoE with LoRA Experts (MoE-LoRA) vs. Mixture of Rank-1 Atomic Memory (MoRAM). Top panel shows MoE-LoRA's three causal pathologies that emerge during training (① intra-expert interference, ② inter-expert redundancy) and manifest at inference (③ routing collapse, leading to catastrophic forgetting). Bottom panel shows MoRAM's three benefits, each mitigating one MoE-LoRA pathology: ① knowledge reuse, ② per-atom specialization, ③ forgetting mitigation.

text.tspan: vs. Mixture of Rank-1 Atomic Memory (MoRAM)

svg.text: MoE-LoRA · adds new sets of rank-r experts for each task
Each new task contributes 2 rank-r experts (A ∈ ℝr × d, B ∈ ℝd × r, r = 4); router R(x) assigns experts — granularity is whole-expert
(1 of 4 ranks matches; 3 co-fire as noise)
⇒ catastrophic forgetting risk on prior-task data
(prior-task data may be rerouted to recently-trained experts)
MoRAM · linear associative memory of rank-1 key-value atoms
Each new task appends 4 rank-1 memory entries (Ai ∈ ℝ1 × d key, Bi ∈ ℝd × 1 value); content-addressable retrieval via Ai · x — no router
③ forgetting mitigation — frozen Ai, Bi preserve prior-task atom routing
Self-activation: each Ai autonomously scores x

g.text: si = Ai · x for all i
every atom's own key Ai scores x in parallel — no centralised router
rank-r expert (A ∈ ℝr × d, B ∈ ℝd × r)
